You are working on the Kotlin/kotlinx.coroutines repository.
The codebase is at version 1.8 (commit: 259000fa).

## Issue to Resolve

Coroutine on EventLoop dispatcher fails to yield to a task which is resuming after a delay
```kotlin
fun main() = runBlocking {
  launch {
    delay(1)
    println("This should be first")
  }
  Thread.sleep(1000)
  yield()
  println("This should be second")
}
```

### Actual behaviour
The code outputs:
```
This should be second
This should be first
```
The call to `yield()` resumes immediately, instead of yielding control to the other coroutine.

This seems to be an issue with the `EventLoop` dispatcher used by `runBlocking`. It maintains a separate queue for delayed continuations. On each iteration of the event loop, tasks whose delay has expired are moved from the delay queue to the main event queue. During a call to `yield()`, this takes place _after_ the `yield()` function has added its own continuation to the event queue. That means a `yield()` call will always fail to yield if the only other continuations waiting to resume are ones whose delay has expired since the last dispatch.

### Expected behaviour
Output should be:
```
This should be first
This should be second
```
On reaching the call to `yield()`, the `launch` job is already eligible to resume. 1 second has elapsed, which is much longer than the 1ms required delay. The dispatcher should prefer to dispatch the `launch` job, instead of resuming the same coroutine that called `yield()`.

### Environment

Reproduced using Kotlin 1.9 with coroutines 1.8.1, on JVM 1.8.

This issue was first reported by a user on Slack: https://kotlinlang.slack.com/archives/C0B8MA7FA/p1716495410746749

### Environment

This issue was first reported by a user on Slack: https://kotlinlang.slack.com/archives/C0B8MA7FA/p1716495410746749

## Additional Context from Issue Discussion

Here's the sequence of events:
* A new coroutine is launched.
* `Thread.sleep`.
* `yield`.
* Now, the thread can start the coroutine that was `launch`-ed.
* `delay` is called, suspending the new coroutine.
* While the new coroutine is suspended, the old one prints the text.
* The new coroutine wakes up and also prints its text.

**That said**, this example *should* work the way you describe:

```kotlin
import kotlinx.coroutines.*

fun main() = runBlocking {
  launch(start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
    delay(1)
    println("This should be first")
  }
  Thread.sleep(1000)
  yield()
  println("This should be second")
}
```

Yet it, too, doesn't.

So, it looks like there is a real bug here, but at the same time, the example you've submitted *is* working as intended.
Oops! I guess when I was simplifying my reproducer code, I accidentally simplified it too much. Thank you for fixing my mistake. Your explanation makes complete sense and I can now see why the code I shared is actually not demonstrating the bug ðŸ¤¦.
The reason behind that behaviour is that internally, there are two event loops -- one is the regular tasks queue, and the other one is the timed tasks queue.

When a timed event fires (meaning it becomes eligible for execution), it is moved to the tasks queue first (which is FIFO), and then the task queue is processed. 

In theory, we can workaround that by nano-timestamping each event and providing some semi-strict ordering, or by checking timed queue on each regular enqueue, but we should be really careful about the consequences 
Actually, @roomscape deserves credit for having already identified this: the same analysis is present in the initial message as well. Thanks for the well-researched issue report!
I took a shortcut and looked at the minified example and expected/actual results first, and then immediately started to think about the reasons ðŸ˜…

Thanks, @roomscape, indeed!

294â†’    @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST")
295â†’    private fun enqueueImpl(task: Runnable): Boolean {
296â†’        _queue.loop { queue ->
297â†’            if (isCompleted) return false // fail fast if already completed, may still add, but queues will close
298â†’            when (queue) {
299â†’                null -> if (_queue.compareAndSet(null, task)) return true
300â†’                is Queue<*> -> {
301â†’                    when ((queue as Queue<Runnable>).addLast(task)) {
302â†’                        Queue.ADD_SUCCESS -> return true
303â†’                        Queue.ADD_CLOSED -> return false
304â†’                        Queue.ADD_FROZEN -> _queue.compareAndSet(queue, queue.next())

309â†’                    else -> {

311â†’                        val newQueue = Queue<Runnable>(Queue.INITIAL_CAPACITY, singleConsumer = true)
312â†’                        newQueue.addLast(queue as Runnable)

323â†’        _queue.loop { queue ->
324â†’            when (queue) {
325â†’                null -> return null

327â†’                    val result = (queue as Queue<Runnable>).removeFirstOrNull()
328â†’                    if (result !== Queue.REMOVE_FROZEN) return result as Runnable?

333â†’                    else -> if (_queue.compareAndSet(queue, null)) return queue as Runnable

340â†’        assert { isCompleted }
341â†’        _queue.loop { queue ->
342â†’            when (queue) {
343â†’                null -> if (_queue.compareAndSet(null, CLOSED_EMPTY)) return
344â†’                is Queue<*> -> {
345â†’                    queue.close()
346â†’                    return
347â†’                }

362â†’    fun schedule(now: Long, delayedTask: DelayedTask) {

374â†’        if (isCompleted) return SCHEDULE_COMPLETED

376â†’            _delayed.compareAndSet(null, DelayedTaskQueue(now))
377â†’            _delayed.value!!

389â†’    private fun rescheduleAllDelayed() {
390â†’        val now = nanoTime()
391â†’        while (true) {
392â†’            /*
393â†’             * `removeFirstOrNull` below is the only operation on DelayedTask & ThreadSafeHeap that is not
394â†’             * synchronized on DelayedTask itself. All other operation are synchronized both on
395â†’             * DelayedTask & ThreadSafeHeap instances (in this order). It is still safe, because `dispose`
396â†’             * first removes DelayedTask from the heap (under synchronization) then
397â†’             * assign "_heap = DISPOSED_TASK", so there cannot be ever a race to _heap reference update.
398â†’             */

404â†’    internal abstract class DelayedTask(
405â†’        /**
406â†’         * This field can be only modified in [scheduleTask] before putting this DelayedTask
407â†’         * into heap to avoid overflow and corruption of heap data structure.
408â†’         */
409â†’        @JvmField var nanoTime: Long
410â†’    ) : Runnable, Comparable<DelayedTask>, DisposableHandle, ThreadSafeHeapNode, SynchronizedObject() {
411â†’        @Volatile
412â†’        private var _heap: Any? = null // null | ThreadSafeHeap | DISPOSED_TASK
413â†’
414â†’        override var heap: ThreadSafeHeap<*>?
415â†’            get() = _heap as? ThreadSafeHeap<*>
416â†’            set(value) {
417â†’                require(_heap !== DISPOSED_TASK) // this can never happen, it is always checked before adding/removing
418â†’                _heap = value
419â†’            }
420â†’
421â†’        override var index: Int = -1
422â†’
423â†’        override fun compareTo(other: DelayedTask): Int {
424â†’            val dTime = nanoTime - other.nanoTime
425â†’            return when {
426â†’                dTime > 0 -> 1
427â†’                dTime < 0 -> -1
428â†’                else -> 0
429â†’            }
430â†’        }
431â†’
432â†’        fun timeToExecute(now: Long): Boolean = now - nanoTime >= 0L
433â†’
434â†’        fun scheduleTask(now: Long, delayed: DelayedTaskQueue, eventLoop: EventLoopImplBase): Int = synchronized<Int>(this) {
435â†’            if (_heap === DISPOSED_TASK) return SCHEDULE_DISPOSED // don't add -- was already disposed
436â†’            delayed.addLastIf(this) { firstTask ->
437â†’                if (eventLoop.isCompleted) return SCHEDULE_COMPLETED // non-local return from scheduleTask
438â†’                /**
439â†’                 * We are about to add new task and we have to make sure that [DelayedTaskQueue]
440â†’                 * invariant is maintained. The code in this lambda is additionally executed under
441â†’                 * the lock of [DelayedTaskQueue] and working with [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] here is thread-safe.
442â†’                 */
443â†’                if (firstTask == null) {
444â†’                    /**
445â†’                     * When adding the first delayed task we simply update queue's [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] to
446â†’                     * the current now time even if that means "going backwards in time". This makes the structure
447â†’                     * self-correcting in spite of wild jumps in `nanoTime()` measurements once all delayed tasks
448â†’                     * are removed from the delayed queue for execution.
449â†’                     */
450â†’                    delayed.timeNow = now
451â†’                } else {
452â†’                    /**
453â†’                     * Carefully update [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] so that it does not sweep past first's tasks time
454â†’                     * and only goes forward in time. We cannot let it go backwards in time or invariant can be
455â†’                     * violated for tasks that were already scheduled.
456â†’                     */
457â†’                    val firstTime = firstTask.nanoTime
458â†’                    // compute min(now, firstTime) using a wrap-safe check
459â†’                    val minTime = if (firstTime - now >= 0) now else firstTime
460â†’                    // update timeNow only when going forward in time
461â†’                    if (minTime - delayed.timeNow > 0) delayed.timeNow = minTime
462â†’                }
463â†’                /**
464â†’                 * Here [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] was already modified and we have to double-check that newly added
465â†’                 * task does not violate [DelayedTaskQueue] invariant because of that. Note also that this scheduleTask
466â†’                 * function can be called to reschedule from one queue to another and this might be another reason
467â†’                 * where new task's time might now violate invariant.
468â†’                 * We correct invariant violation (if any) by simply changing this task's time to now.
469â†’                 */
470â†’                if (nanoTime - delayed.timeNow < 0) nanoTime = delayed.timeNow
471â†’                true
472â†’            }
473â†’            return SCHEDULE_OK
474â†’        }
475â†’
476â†’        final override fun dispose(): Unit = synchronized(this) {
477â†’            val heap = _heap
478â†’            if (heap === DISPOSED_TASK) return // already disposed
479â†’            (heap as? DelayedTaskQueue)?.remove(this) // remove if it is in heap (first)
480â†’            _heap = DISPOSED_TASK // never add again to any heap
481â†’        }
482â†’
483â†’        override fun toString(): String = "Delayed[nanos=$nanoTime]"
484â†’    }

486â†’    private inner class DelayedResumeTask(
487â†’        nanoTime: Long,
488â†’        private val cont: CancellableContinuation<Unit>
489â†’    ) : DelayedTask(nanoTime) {
490â†’        override fun run() { with(cont) { resumeUndispatched(Unit) } }
491â†’        override fun toString(): String = super.toString() + cont.toString()

494â†’    private class DelayedRunnableTask(
495â†’        nanoTime: Long,
496â†’        private val block: Runnable
497â†’    ) : DelayedTask(nanoTime) {
498â†’        override fun run() { block.run() }

502â†’    /**
503â†’     * Delayed task queue maintains stable time-comparision invariant despite potential wraparounds in
504â†’     * long nano time measurements by maintaining last observed [timeNow]. It protects the integrity of the
505â†’     * heap data structure in spite of potential non-monotonicity of `nanoTime()` source.
506â†’     * The invariant is that for every scheduled [DelayedTask]:
507â†’     *
508â†’     * ```
509â†’     * delayedTask.nanoTime - timeNow >= 0
510â†’     * ```
511â†’     *
512â†’     * So the comparison of scheduled tasks via [DelayedTask.compareTo] is always stable as
513â†’     * scheduled [DelayedTask.nanoTime] can be at most [Long.MAX_VALUE] apart. This invariant is maintained when
514â†’     * new tasks are added by [DelayedTask.scheduleTask] function and it cannot be violated when tasks are removed
515â†’     * (so there is nothing special to do there).
516â†’     */
517â†’    internal class DelayedTaskQueue(
518â†’        @JvmField var timeNow: Long
519â†’    ) : ThreadSafeHeap<DelayedTask>()
520â†’}

1â†’package kotlinx.coroutines
 2â†’
 3â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.internal.*
 4â†’import kotlin.coroutines.intrinsics.*
 5â†’
 6â†’/**
 7â†’ * Yields the thread (or thread pool) of the current coroutine dispatcher
 8â†’ * to other coroutines on the same dispatcher to run if possible.
 9â†’ *
10â†’ * This suspending function is cancellable: if the [Job] of the current coroutine is cancelled while
11â†’ * [yield] is invoked or while waiting for dispatch, it immediately resumes with [CancellationException].
12â†’ * There is a **prompt cancellation guarantee**: even if this function is ready to return the result, but was cancelled
13â†’ * while suspended, [CancellationException] will be thrown. See [suspendCancellableCoroutine] for low-level details.
14â†’ *
15â†’ * **Note**: This function always [checks for cancellation][ensureActive] even when it does not suspend.
16â†’ *
17â†’ * ### Implementation details
18â†’ *
19â†’ * If the coroutine dispatcher is [Unconfined][Dispatchers.Unconfined], this
20â†’ * functions suspends only when there are other unconfined coroutines working and forming an event-loop.
21â†’ * For other dispatchers, this function calls [CoroutineDispatcher.dispatch] and
22â†’ * always suspends to be resumed later regardless of the result of [CoroutineDispatcher.isDispatchNeeded].
23â†’ * If there is no [CoroutineDispatcher] in the context, it does not suspend.
24â†’ */
25â†’public suspend fun yield(): Unit = suspendCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn sc@ { uCont ->
26â†’    val context = uCont.context
27â†’    context.ensureActive()
28â†’    val cont = uCont.intercepted() as? DispatchedContinuation<Unit> ?: return@sc Unit
29â†’    if (cont.dispatcher.isDispatchNeeded(context)) {
30â†’        // this is a regular dispatcher -- do simple dispatchYield
31â†’        cont.dispatchYield(context, Unit)
32â†’    } else {
33â†’        // This is either an "immediate" dispatcher or the Unconfined dispatcher
34â†’        // This code detects the Unconfined dispatcher even if it was wrapped into another dispatcher
35â†’        val yieldContext = YieldContext()
36â†’        cont.dispatchYield(context + yieldContext, Unit)
37â†’        // Special case for the unconfined dispatcher that can yield only in existing unconfined loop
38â†’        if (yieldContext.dispatcherWasUnconfined) {
39â†’            // Means that the Unconfined dispatcher got the call, but did not do anything.
40â†’            // See also code of "Unconfined.dispatch" function.
41â†’            return@sc if (cont.yieldUndispatched()) COROUTINE_SUSPENDED else Unit
42â†’        }
43â†’        // Otherwise, it was some other dispatcher that successfully dispatched the coroutine
44â†’    }
45â†’    COROUTINE_SUSPENDED
46â†’}
47â†’

1â†’package kotlinx.coroutines.internal
  2â†’
  3â†’import kotlinx.atomicfu.*
  4â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
  5â†’import kotlin.coroutines.*
  6â†’import kotlin.jvm.*
  7â†’
  8â†’private val UNDEFINED = Symbol("UNDEFINED")
  9â†’@JvmField
 10â†’internal val REUSABLE_CLAIMED = Symbol("REUSABLE_CLAIMED")
 11â†’
 12â†’internal class DispatchedContinuation<in T>(
 13â†’    @JvmField internal val dispatcher: CoroutineDispatcher,
 14â†’    @JvmField val continuation: Continuation<T>
 15â†’) : DispatchedTask<T>(MODE_UNINITIALIZED), CoroutineStackFrame, Continuation<T> by continuation {
 16â†’    @JvmField
 17â†’    @Suppress("PropertyName")
 18â†’    internal var _state: Any? = UNDEFINED
 19â†’    override val callerFrame: CoroutineStackFrame? get() = continuation as? CoroutineStackFrame
 20â†’    override fun getStackTraceElement(): StackTraceElement? = null
 21â†’    @JvmField // pre-cached value to avoid ctx.fold on every resumption
 22â†’    internal val countOrElement = threadContextElements(context)
 23â†’
 24â†’    /**
 25â†’     * Possible states of reusability:
 26â†’     *
 27â†’     * 1) `null`. Cancellable continuation wasn't yet attempted to be reused or
 28â†’     *     was used and then invalidated (e.g. because of the cancellation).
 29â†’     * 2) [CancellableContinuation]. Continuation to be/that is being reused.
 30â†’     * 3) [REUSABLE_CLAIMED]. CC is currently being reused and its owner executes `suspend` block:
 31â†’     *    ```
 32â†’     *    // state == null | CC
 33â†’     *    suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable { cont ->
 34â†’     *        // state == REUSABLE_CLAIMED
 35â†’     *        block(cont)
 36â†’     *    }
 37â†’     *    // state == CC
 38â†’     *    ```
 39â†’     * 4) [Throwable] continuation was cancelled with this cause while being in [suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable],
 40â†’     *    [CancellableContinuationImpl.getResult] will check for cancellation later.
 41â†’     *
 42â†’     * [REUSABLE_CLAIMED] state is required to prevent double-use of the reused continuation.
 43â†’     * In the `getResult`, we have the following code:
 44â†’     * ```
 45â†’     * if (trySuspend()) {
 46â†’     *     // <- at this moment current continuation can be redispatched and claimed again.
 47â†’     *     attachChildToParent()
 48â†’     *     releaseClaimedContinuation()
 49â†’     * }
 50â†’     * ```
 51â†’     */
 52â†’    private val _reusableCancellableContinuation = atomic<Any?>(null)
 53â†’
 54â†’    private val reusableCancellableContinuation: CancellableContinuationImpl<*>?
 55â†’        get() = _reusableCancellableContinuation.value as? CancellableContinuationImpl<*>
 56â†’
 57â†’    internal fun isReusable(): Boolean {
 58â†’        /*
 59â†’        Invariant: caller.resumeMode.isReusableMode
 60â†’         * Reusability control:
 61â†’         * `null` -> no reusability at all, `false`
 62â†’         * anything else -> reusable.
 63â†’         */
 64â†’        return _reusableCancellableContinuation.value != null
 65â†’    }
 66â†’
 67â†’    /**
 68â†’     * Awaits until previous call to `suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable` will
 69â†’     * stop mutating cached instance
 70â†’     */
 71â†’    internal fun awaitReusability() {
 72â†’        _reusableCancellableContinuation.loop {
 73â†’            if (it !== REUSABLE_CLAIMED) return
 74â†’        }
 75â†’    }
 76â†’
 77â†’    internal fun release() {
 78â†’        /*
 79â†’         * Called from `releaseInterceptedContinuation`, can be concurrent with
 80â†’         * the code in `getResult` right after `trySuspend` returned `true`, so we have
 81â†’         * to wait for a release here.
 82â†’         */
 83â†’        awaitReusability()
 84â†’        reusableCancellableContinuation?.detachChild()
 85â†’    }
 86â†’
 87â†’    /**
 88â†’     * Claims the continuation for [suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable] block,
 89â†’     * so all cancellations will be postponed.
 90â†’     */
 91â†’    @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST")
 92â†’    internal fun claimReusableCancellableContinuation(): CancellableContinuationImpl<T>? {
 93â†’        /*
 94â†’         * Transitions:
 95â†’         * 1) `null` -> claimed, caller will instantiate CC instance
 96â†’         * 2) `CC` -> claimed, caller will reuse CC instance
 97â†’         */
 98â†’        _reusableCancellableContinuation.loop { state ->
 99â†’            when {
100â†’                state === null -> {
101â†’                    /*
102â†’                     * null -> CC was not yet published -> we do not compete with cancel
103â†’                     * -> can use plain store instead of CAS
104â†’                     */
105â†’                    _reusableCancellableContinuation.value = REUSABLE_CLAIMED
106â†’                    return null
107â†’                }
108â†’                // potentially competing with cancel
109â†’                state is CancellableContinuationImpl<*> -> {
110â†’                    if (_reusableCancellableContinuation.compareAndSet(state, REUSABLE_CLAIMED)) {
111â†’                        return state as CancellableContinuationImpl<T>
112â†’                    }
113â†’                }
114â†’                state === REUSABLE_CLAIMED -> {
115â†’                    // Do nothing, wait until reusable instance will be returned from
116â†’                    // getResult() of a previous `suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable`
117â†’                }
118â†’                state is Throwable -> {
119â†’                    // Also do nothing, Throwable can only indicate that the CC
120â†’                    // is in REUSABLE_CLAIMED state, but with postponed cancellation
121â†’                }
122â†’                else -> error("Inconsistent state $state")
123â†’            }
124â†’        }
125â†’    }
126â†’
127â†’    /**
128â†’     * Checks whether there were any attempts to cancel reusable CC while it was in [REUSABLE_CLAIMED] state
129â†’     * and returns cancellation cause if so, `null` otherwise.
130â†’     * If continuation was cancelled, it becomes non-reusable.
131â†’     *
132â†’     * ```
133â†’     * suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable { // <- claimed
134â†’     * // Any asynchronous cancellation is "postponed" while this block
135â†’     * // is being executed
136â†’     * } // postponed cancellation is checked here in `getResult`
137â†’     * ```
138â†’     *
139â†’     * See [CancellableContinuationImpl.getResult].
140â†’     */
141â†’    internal fun tryReleaseClaimedContinuation(continuation: CancellableContinuation<*>): Throwable? {
142â†’        _reusableCancellableContinuation.loop { state ->
143â†’            // not when(state) to avoid Intrinsics.equals call
144â†’            when {
145â†’                state === REUSABLE_CLAIMED -> {
146â†’                    if (_reusableCancellableContinuation.compareAndSet(REUSABLE_CLAIMED, continuation)) return null
147â†’                }
148â†’                state is Throwable -> {
149â†’                    require(_reusableCancellableContinuation.compareAndSet(state, null))
150â†’                    return state
151â†’                }
152â†’                else -> error("Inconsistent state $state")
153â†’            }
154â†’        }
155â†’    }
156â†’
157â†’    /**
158â†’     * Tries to postpone cancellation if reusable CC is currently in [REUSABLE_CLAIMED] state.
159â†’     * Returns `true` if cancellation is (or previously was) postponed, `false` otherwise.

  7â†’ * Base class to be extended by all coroutine dispatcher implementations.
  8â†’ *
  9â†’ * The following standard implementations are provided by `kotlinx.coroutines` as properties on
 10â†’ * the [Dispatchers] object:
 11â†’ *
 12â†’ * - [Dispatchers.Default] — is used by all standard builders if no dispatcher or any other [ContinuationInterceptor]
 13â†’ *   is specified in their context. It uses a common pool of shared background threads.
 14â†’ *   This is an appropriate choice for compute-intensive coroutines that consume CPU resources.
 15â†’ * - [Dispatchers.IO] — uses a shared pool of on-demand created threads and is designed for offloading of IO-intensive _blocking_
 16â†’ *   operations (like file I/O and blocking socket I/O).
 17â†’ * - [Dispatchers.Unconfined] — starts coroutine execution in the current call-frame until the first suspension,
 18â†’ *   whereupon the coroutine builder function returns.
 19â†’ *   The coroutine will later resume in whatever thread used by the
 20â†’ *   corresponding suspending function, without confining it to any specific thread or pool.
 21â†’ *   **The `Unconfined` dispatcher should not normally be used in code**.
 22â†’ * - Private thread pools can be created with [newSingleThreadContext] and [newFixedThreadPoolContext].
 23â†’ * - An arbitrary [Executor][java.util.concurrent.Executor] can be converted to a dispatcher with the [asCoroutineDispatcher] extension function.
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * This class ensures that debugging facilities in [newCoroutineContext] function work properly.
 26â†’ */

 39â†’     *

 41â†’     * potentially forming an event-loop to prevent stack overflows.
 42â†’     * The event loop is an advanced topic and its implications can be found in [Dispatchers.Unconfined] documentation.
 43â†’     *
 44â†’     * The [context] parameter represents the context of the coroutine that is being dispatched,
 45â†’     * or [EmptyCoroutineContext] if a non-coroutine-specific [Runnable] is dispatched instead.
 46â†’     *
 47â†’     * A dispatcher can override this method to provide a performance optimization and avoid paying a cost of an unnecessary dispatch.
 48â†’     * E.g. [MainCoroutineDispatcher.immediate] checks whether we are already in the required UI thread in this method and avoids
 49â†’     * an additional dispatch when it is not required.
 50â†’     *

 52â†’     * so that users won't observe unexpected and non-consistent order of events by default.

 59â†’     * may leave the coroutines that use this dispatcher in the inconsistent and hard to debug state.
 60â†’     *
 61â†’     * @see dispatch
 62â†’     * @see Dispatchers.Unconfined
 63â†’     */

 66â†’    /**
 67â†’     * Creates a view of the current dispatcher that limits the parallelism to the given [value][parallelism].
 68â†’     * The resulting view uses the original dispatcher for execution but with the guarantee that
 69â†’     * no more than [parallelism] coroutines are executed at the same time.
 70â†’     *
 71â†’     * This method does not impose restrictions on the number of views or the total sum of parallelism values,
 72â†’     * each view controls its own parallelism independently with the guarantee that the effective parallelism
 73â†’     * of all views cannot exceed the actual parallelism of the original dispatcher.
 74â†’     *

 76â†’     * subset of threads, it only guarantees that at most [parallelism] coroutines are executed at the same time,
 77â†’     * and reuses threads from the original dispatchers.
 78â†’     * It does not constitute a resource -- it is a _view_ of the underlying dispatcher that can be thrown away
 79â†’     * and is not required to be closed.
 80â†’     *
 81â†’     * ### Example of usage
 82â†’     * ```

 94â†’     * and at most 4 threads can exist in the system.

177â†’internal abstract class EventLoopImplBase: EventLoopImplPlatform(), Delay {
178â†’    // null | CLOSED_EMPTY | task | Queue<Runnable>
179â†’    private val _queue = atomic<Any?>(null)

181â†’    // Allocated only only once
182â†’    private val _delayed = atomic<DelayedTaskQueue?>(null)
183â†’
184â†’    private val _isCompleted = atomic(false)
185â†’    private var isCompleted
186â†’        get() = _isCompleted.value
187â†’        set(value) { _isCompleted.value = value }
188â†’
189â†’    override val isEmpty: Boolean get() {
190â†’        if (!isUnconfinedQueueEmpty) return false
191â†’        val delayed = _delayed.value
192â†’        if (delayed != null && !delayed.isEmpty) return false
193â†’        return when (val queue = _queue.value) {
194â†’            null -> true
195â†’            is Queue<*> -> queue.isEmpty
196â†’            else -> queue === CLOSED_EMPTY
197â†’        }
198â†’    }
199â†’
200â†’    override val nextTime: Long
201â†’        get() {
202â†’            if (super.nextTime == 0L) return 0L
203â†’            val queue = _queue.value
204â†’            when {
205â†’                queue === null -> {} // empty queue -- proceed
206â†’                queue is Queue<*> -> if (!queue.isEmpty) return 0 // non-empty queue
207â†’                queue === CLOSED_EMPTY -> return Long.MAX_VALUE // no more events -- closed
208â†’                else -> return 0 // non-empty queue
209â†’            }
210â†’            val nextDelayedTask = _delayed.value?.peek() ?: return Long.MAX_VALUE
211â†’            return (nextDelayedTask.nanoTime - nanoTime()).coerceAtLeast(0)

214â†’    override fun shutdown() {

216â†’        ThreadLocalEventLoop.resetEventLoop()
217â†’        // We should signal that this event loop should not accept any more tasks
218â†’        // and process queued events (that could have been added after last processNextEvent)
219â†’        isCompleted = true
220â†’        closeQueue()

222â†’        while (processNextEvent() <= 0) { /* spin */ }

227â†’    override fun scheduleResumeAfterDelay(timeMillis: Long, continuation: CancellableContinuation<Unit>) {
228â†’        val timeNanos = delayToNanos(timeMillis)
229â†’        if (timeNanos < MAX_DELAY_NS) {
230â†’            val now = nanoTime()
231â†’            DelayedResumeTask(now + timeNanos, continuation).also { task ->
232â†’                /*
233â†’                 * Order is important here: first we schedule the heap and only then
234â†’                 * publish it to continuation. Otherwise, `DelayedResumeTask` would
235â†’                 * have to know how to be disposed of even when it wasn't scheduled yet.

244â†’        val timeNanos = delayToNanos(timeMillis)
245â†’        return if (timeNanos < MAX_DELAY_NS) {

255â†’    override fun processNextEvent(): Long {
256â†’        // unconfined events take priority
257â†’        if (processUnconfinedEvent()) return 0
258â†’        // queue all delayed tasks that are due to be executed
259â†’        val delayed = _delayed.value
260â†’        if (delayed != null && !delayed.isEmpty) {
261â†’            val now = nanoTime()
262â†’            while (true) {
263â†’                // make sure that moving from delayed to queue removes from delayed only after it is added to queue

296â†’        _queue.loop { queue ->
297â†’            if (isCompleted) return false // fail fast if already completed, may still add, but queues will close

299â†’                null -> if (_queue.compareAndSet(null, task)) return true

316â†’                }
317â†’            }
318â†’        }

324â†’            when (queue) {
325â†’                null -> return null

342â†’            when (queue) {

392â†’            /*

404â†’    internal abstract class DelayedTask(
405â†’        /**
406â†’         * This field can be only modified in [scheduleTask] before putting this DelayedTask
407â†’         * into heap to avoid overflow and corruption of heap data structure.
408â†’         */
409â†’        @JvmField var nanoTime: Long
410â†’    ) : Runnable, Comparable<DelayedTask>, DisposableHandle, ThreadSafeHeapNode, SynchronizedObject() {
411â†’        @Volatile
412â†’        private var _heap: Any? = null // null | ThreadSafeHeap | DISPOSED_TASK
413â†’
414â†’        override var heap: ThreadSafeHeap<*>?
415â†’            get() = _heap as? ThreadSafeHeap<*>
416â†’            set(value) {
417â†’                require(_heap !== DISPOSED_TASK) // this can never happen, it is always checked before adding/removing
418â†’                _heap = value
419â†’            }
420â†’
421â†’        override var index: Int = -1
422â†’
423â†’        override fun compareTo(other: DelayedTask): Int {
424â†’            val dTime = nanoTime - other.nanoTime
425â†’            return when {
426â†’                dTime > 0 -> 1
427â†’                dTime < 0 -> -1
428â†’                else -> 0
429â†’            }
430â†’        }
431â†’
432â†’        fun timeToExecute(now: Long): Boolean = now - nanoTime >= 0L
433â†’
434â†’        fun scheduleTask(now: Long, delayed: DelayedTaskQueue, eventLoop: EventLoopImplBase): Int = synchronized<Int>(this) {
435â†’            if (_heap === DISPOSED_TASK) return SCHEDULE_DISPOSED // don't add -- was already disposed
436â†’            delayed.addLastIf(this) { firstTask ->
437â†’                if (eventLoop.isCompleted) return SCHEDULE_COMPLETED // non-local return from scheduleTask
438â†’                /**
439â†’                 * We are about to add new task and we have to make sure that [DelayedTaskQueue]
440â†’                 * invariant is maintained. The code in this lambda is additionally executed under
441â†’                 * the lock of [DelayedTaskQueue] and working with [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] here is thread-safe.
442â†’                 */
443â†’                if (firstTask == null) {
444â†’                    /**
445â†’                     * When adding the first delayed task we simply update queue's [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] to
446â†’                     * the current now time even if that means "going backwards in time". This makes the structure
447â†’                     * self-correcting in spite of wild jumps in `nanoTime()` measurements once all delayed tasks
448â†’                     * are removed from the delayed queue for execution.
449â†’                     */
450â†’                    delayed.timeNow = now
451â†’                } else {
452â†’                    /**
453â†’                     * Carefully update [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] so that it does not sweep past first's tasks time
454â†’                     * and only goes forward in time. We cannot let it go backwards in time or invariant can be
455â†’                     * violated for tasks that were already scheduled.
456â†’                     */
457â†’                    val firstTime = firstTask.nanoTime
458â†’                    // compute min(now, firstTime) using a wrap-safe check
459â†’                    val minTime = if (firstTime - now >= 0) now else firstTime
460â†’                    // update timeNow only when going forward in time
461â†’                    if (minTime - delayed.timeNow > 0) delayed.timeNow = minTime
462â†’                }
463â†’                /**
464â†’                 * Here [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] was already modified and we have to double-check that newly added
465â†’                 * task does not violate [DelayedTaskQueue] invariant because of that. Note also that this scheduleTask
466â†’                 * function can be called to reschedule from one queue to another and this might be another reason
467â†’                 * where new task's time might now violate invariant.
468â†’                 * We correct invariant violation (if any) by simply changing this task's time to now.
469â†’                 */
470â†’                if (nanoTime - delayed.timeNow < 0) nanoTime = delayed.timeNow
471â†’                true
472â†’            }
473â†’            return SCHEDULE_OK
474â†’        }
475â†’
476â†’        final override fun dispose(): Unit = synchronized(this) {
477â†’            val heap = _heap
478â†’            if (heap === DISPOSED_TASK) return // already disposed
479â†’            (heap as? DelayedTaskQueue)?.remove(this) // remove if it is in heap (first)
480â†’            _heap = DISPOSED_TASK // never add again to any heap
481â†’        }
482â†’
483â†’        override fun toString(): String = "Delayed[nanos=$nanoTime]"
484â†’    }

505â†’     * heap data structure in spite of potential non-monotonicity of `nanoTime()` source.
506â†’     * The invariant is that for every scheduled [DelayedTask]:

513â†’     * scheduled [DelayedTask.nanoTime] can be at most [Long.MAX_VALUE] apart. This invariant is maintained when
514â†’     * new tasks are added by [DelayedTask.scheduleTask] function and it cannot be violated when tasks are removed
515â†’     * (so there is nothing special to do there).

518â†’        @JvmField var timeNow: Long
519â†’    ) : ThreadSafeHeap<DelayedTask>()
520â†’}

 5â†’
 6â†’/**
 7â†’ * Yields the thread (or thread pool) of the current coroutine dispatcher
 8â†’ * to other coroutines on the same dispatcher to run if possible.
 9â†’ *
10â†’ * This suspending function is cancellable: if the [Job] of the current coroutine is cancelled while
11â†’ * [yield] is invoked or while waiting for dispatch, it immediately resumes with [CancellationException].
12â†’ * There is a **prompt cancellation guarantee**: even if this function is ready to return the result, but was cancelled
13â†’ * while suspended, [CancellationException] will be thrown. See [suspendCancellableCoroutine] for low-level details.
14â†’ *
15â†’ * **Note**: This function always [checks for cancellation][ensureActive] even when it does not suspend.
16â†’ *
17â†’ * ### Implementation details
18â†’ *
19â†’ * If the coroutine dispatcher is [Unconfined][Dispatchers.Unconfined], this
20â†’ * functions suspends only when there are other unconfined coroutines working and forming an event-loop.
21â†’ * For other dispatchers, this function calls [CoroutineDispatcher.dispatch] and
22â†’ * always suspends to be resumed later regardless of the result of [CoroutineDispatcher.isDispatchNeeded].
23â†’ * If there is no [CoroutineDispatcher] in the context, it does not suspend.
24â†’ */
25â†’public suspend fun yield(): Unit = suspendCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn sc@ { uCont ->
26â†’    val context = uCont.context
27â†’    context.ensureActive()
28â†’    val cont = uCont.intercepted() as? DispatchedContinuation<Unit> ?: return@sc Unit
29â†’    if (cont.dispatcher.isDispatchNeeded(context)) {
30â†’        // this is a regular dispatcher -- do simple dispatchYield
31â†’        cont.dispatchYield(context, Unit)
32â†’    } else {
33â†’        // This is either an "immediate" dispatcher or the Unconfined dispatcher
34â†’        // This code detects the Unconfined dispatcher even if it was wrapped into another dispatcher
35â†’        val yieldContext = YieldContext()
36â†’        cont.dispatchYield(context + yieldContext, Unit)
37â†’        // Special case for the unconfined dispatcher that can yield only in existing unconfined loop
38â†’        if (yieldContext.dispatcherWasUnconfined) {
39â†’            // Means that the Unconfined dispatcher got the call, but did not do anything.
40â†’            // See also code of "Unconfined.dispatch" function.
41â†’            return@sc if (cont.yieldUndispatched()) COROUTINE_SUSPENDED else Unit
42â†’        }
43â†’        // Otherwise, it was some other dispatcher that successfully dispatched the coroutine
44â†’    }
45â†’    COROUTINE_SUSPENDED
46â†’}

 29â†’     * 2) [CancellableContinuation]. Continuation to be/that is being reused.
 30â†’     * 3) [REUSABLE_CLAIMED]. CC is currently being reused and its owner executes `suspend` block:
 31â†’     *    ```
 32â†’     *    // state == null | CC
 33â†’     *    suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable { cont ->
 34â†’     *        // state == REUSABLE_CLAIMED
 35â†’     *        block(cont)
 36â†’     *    }
 37â†’     *    // state == CC
 38â†’     *    ```

285â†’/**
286â†’ * Executes given [block] as part of current event loop, updating current continuation
287â†’ * mode and state if continuation is not resumed immediately.
288â†’ * [doYield] indicates whether current continuation is yielding (to provide fast-path if event-loop is empty).
289â†’ * Returns `true` if execution of continuation was queued (trampolined) or `false` otherwise.

291â†’private inline fun DispatchedContinuation<*>.executeUnconfined(
292â†’    contState: Any?, mode: Int, doYield: Boolean = false,
293â†’    block: () -> Unit
294â†’): Boolean {
295â†’    assert { mode != MODE_UNINITIALIZED } // invalid execution mode
296â†’    val eventLoop = ThreadLocalEventLoop.eventLoop
297â†’    // If we are yielding and unconfined queue is empty, we can bail out as part of fast path
298â†’    if (doYield && eventLoop.isUnconfinedQueueEmpty) return false
299â†’    return if (eventLoop.isUnconfinedLoopActive) {
300â†’        // When unconfined loop is active -- dispatch continuation for execution to avoid stack overflow
301â†’        _state = contState
302â†’        resumeMode = mode
303â†’        eventLoop.dispatchUnconfined(this)
304â†’        true // queued into the active loop
305â†’    } else {
306â†’        // Was not active -- run event loop until all unconfined tasks are executed
307â†’        runUnconfinedEventLoop(eventLoop, block = block)
308â†’        false
309â†’    }
